blueprint_refs: Motivation for a target day is computed from the past 7 observed days (not necessarily consecutive calendar days)
validation targets must have >=7 prior observed days ... (not necessarily consecutive calendar days)

evidence.metrics: stop_count_abs_mean_error=0.158, stop_count_kl=0.129, tod_jsd_avg=0.0255; code returns default motivation unless ALL of (t-1..t-7) exist.

reflection.error_identification: Motivation conditioning is frequently skipped unless the prior 7 calendar days are all present; this contradicts the spec’s “past 7 observed days” requirement and weakens day-specific stop-count and timing modulation.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: In MobilitySimulator._compute_motivation, it constructs days = [target_day-1 ... target_day-7] and bails out to neutral motivation if any are missing. Blueprint requires selecting the most recent 7 observed days prior to the target date (not necessarily consecutive).
reflection.correct_approach: Replace the consecutive-day check with: collect all observed dates < target_day from agent.observed_2021, take the latest 7 of those, and compute features from them. If <7 exist, either exclude the day (as validation split already does) or fall back to a weaker motivation estimate (e.g., use all available prior days up to 7). Ensure no future leakage by strictly using obs_d < target_day.
reflection.key_insight: When a spec says “last N observed events,” never implement it as “last N calendar days”—select by available observations to avoid silent feature dropout.

blueprint_refs: Parse ... into trajectories (date, ordered visits with POI category, POI id, time)
ordered sequences of (POI_location, time) visits

evidence.metrics: transition_divergence=0.0251 (could be artificially low if ordering is normalized); sample outputs show many repeated identical POIs with small time gaps.

reflection.error_identification: The parser re-sorts visits by time (and stable index), which can destroy the original listed order and collapse/alter adjacent transition structure when multiple events share identical timestamps or when the string order matters.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: parse_day_record builds visits via regex iteration (which preserves textual order), then explicitly sorts by (time_min, original_index). Blueprint requires ordered sequences; for ties, the string order is the authoritative order. Re-sorting also changes training-derived transitions and dwell computations that assume adjacency from the observed sequence.
reflection.correct_approach: Preserve the textual order from VISIT_RE.finditer and only enforce monotonic time during simulation/serialization. If you must handle out-of-order timestamps, prefer a minimal repair: keep order, and clamp non-monotone times to last_t (similar to serialize_day_record) while logging a counter of repaired times.
reflection.key_insight: For sequence models, keep the dataset’s native ordering; do not “normalize” ordering unless the spec explicitly defines a canonical order.

blueprint_refs: U(poi)=alpha_pref*I(poi in memory)+alpha_dist*(-distance(current,poi)) + alpha_anchor*(-distance(anchor,poi)) + epsilon

evidence.metrics: trip_distance_wasserstein=3.174 (largest error among reported metrics); sample outputs show many long, erratic trips and frequent returns to the same anchor-like POI.

reflection.error_identification: Trip distance distribution mismatch remains relatively high, suggesting the spatial utility may be mis-specified and over-regularized toward home/anchor in ways that distort inter-stop distances.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: In _choose_next_poi, anchor is set to home_poi or current_poi, and the utility penalizes both distance from current_poi (alpha_dist) and distance from home_poi (alpha_anchor) simultaneously on every step. If home_poi is also the start/current frequently, this effectively double-counts proximity to home and can distort the distribution of consecutive-stop distances (especially when “home” is included as a stop).
reflection.correct_approach: Follow the blueprint’s intended meaning of anchor: use home/work as an anchor depending on time-of-day/state (e.g., daytime anchor=work if known; otherwise home), and avoid applying both penalties when anchor==current_poi (skip anchor term or treat anchor as a separate fixed target). Additionally, compute travel-time and trip-distance from consecutive non-identical POIs to avoid home-inflated short hops if required by evaluation.
reflection.key_insight: When a utility has multiple distance terms, guard against accidental correlation/double-counting (e.g., current==anchor) to avoid skewing spatial distributions.

blueprint_refs: sample baseline number of stops ... from learned per-user distributions (Pattern)
Motivation ... modulates stop count

evidence.metrics: stop_count_abs_mean_error=0.158 and stop_count_kl=0.129; stop counts match mean fairly well but distribution still diverges.

reflection.error_identification: Stop-count mean error is small but KL divergence is still noticeable, indicating distribution shape mismatch (e.g., too peaky or too heavy-tailed).
reflection.root_cause_analysis: _sample_stop_count builds a categorical distribution from the empirical histogram, then adjusts log-probabilities using math.log(mult) * (c / baseline_mean). This is an ad-hoc tilt that can over-amplify high counts for mult>1 and suppress them for mult<1, changing distribution shape more than intended (especially when baseline_mean is small).
reflection.correct_approach: Use a distributional modulation that preserves the empirical shape better: (a) sample from empirical histogram unmodified, then apply a bounded adjustment (e.g., +/-1 stop with some probability based on motivation), or (b) fit a parametric count model per user (e.g., Poisson/NegBin) and modulate its mean with motivation. Keep min/max feasibility constraints, but avoid log-tilting proportional to count unless calibrated.
reflection.key_insight: Match both mean and distribution: avoid heuristic reweighting that can silently warp tails; prefer interpretable count models or small post-sampling adjustments.

blueprint_refs: trip distance distributions ... join lat/lon via poi_category_192021_longitude_latitude.json

evidence.metrics: trip_distance_wasserstein=3.174; meta shows gt coord_match_rate=0.301 while sim coord_match_rate=0.618 (different missingness).

reflection.error_identification: Ground-truth and simulated trip-distance distributions are computed over different subsets of transitions due to different coordinate match rates, which can bias Wasserstein comparisons and inflate/deflate the metric unrelated to behavioral quality.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: The simulator explicitly prioritizes POIs with coordinates (candidates_for_category and _choose_next_poi reorder pools toward has_coords). Meanwhile, many ground-truth POIs lack coordinates (gt match rate ~0.30). Evaluator._trip_distances drops transitions without coordinates, so the compared distributions are on mismatched supports.
reflection.correct_approach: Align evaluation support: when computing trip distances, restrict both GT and SIM to transitions where BOTH endpoints have coordinates in the shared POI catalog (or alternatively, impute missing coords consistently for both). If you keep the current metric definition, remove the simulator’s coordinate-prioritization bias (or make it match the observed coordinate availability by category/user) to reduce selection effects.
reflection.key_insight: Distance metrics are sensitive to missing geo joins; ensure GT/SIM are evaluated on the same eligible set or apply consistent imputation, otherwise the metric confounds behavior with data coverage.

blueprint_refs: Generate realistic daily mobility trajectories ...
OUTPUT FORMAT STRICT: serialize each simulated day as a single string ... with no extra text/JSON/markdown.

evidence.metrics: Execution succeeded without calling LLM; these classes are dead code unrelated to mobility simulation.

reflection.error_identification: The code includes an OpenAI-based review-writing subsystem that is unrelated to the mobility simulator and not used in the pipeline, increasing complexity and risking environment failures if activated.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Top-of-file LLM integration and ReviewAuthor stack do not participate in main(). This is off-blueprint and could introduce dependency/credential issues if inadvertently called.
reflection.correct_approach: Remove unused LLM/review-agent code paths from this mobility simulator module. If LLM is intended for Persona inference (optional per blueprint), implement it in a bounded, off-by-default component that consumes only pre-target 2021 data and never affects output format.
reflection.key_insight: Keep the simulation artifact minimal: remove dead/orthogonal subsystems to reduce failure surface and maintain spec focus.

blueprint_refs: objective: "Generate realistic daily mobility trajectories..."
OUTPUT FORMAT STRICT: serialize each simulated day as a single string exactly matching the 1921Y.json day-record format

evidence.metrics: execution_status=success (no crash), but large unrelated subsystem exists in CURRENT CODE

reflection.error_identification: Codebase contains a review-writing LLM subsystem unrelated to mobility simulation, increasing dependency and failure surface without contributing to required outputs.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: CURRENT CODE includes OpenAI/Responses API calls and review authoring agents that are not referenced by the mobility pipeline but violate scope and can break runs when OPENAI_API_KEY/openai package is missing (even if not triggered). Location: call_gpt5_with_responses_api + ReasoningAgent stack.
reflection.correct_approach: Remove the entire review/LLM subsystem (OpenAI import, get_openai_api_key, call_gpt5_with_responses_api, MemoryAgent/PlanningAgent/ReasoningAgent/ReviewAuthor) unless explicitly required for mobility Persona inference. If Persona inference is desired later, implement it behind a feature flag and ensure default path is offline and deterministic.
reflection.key_insight: Keep generated simulators single-purpose: delete unused subsystems that introduce external dependencies, even if they are not executed in the main path.

blueprint_refs: "stops-per-day distribution"
"return-home hazard"
"ensure monotonic increase within day"

evidence.metrics: stop_count_abs_mean_error=0.787790697674418, stop_count_kl=0.3852963082198649

reflection.error_identification: Daily stop counts are systematically mismatched (mean error ~0.79 stops/day; KL ~0.39), consistent with the simulator adding extra visits beyond the sampled stop budget.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: generate_day samples stop_count, then may append an extra late-day return-home visit without accounting for the stop budget (it does not decrement/integrate into stop_count). This shifts the simulated stop-count distribution upward and distorts KL. Location: generate_day post-loop 'return home' append.
reflection.correct_approach: Make stop budget binding: either (a) treat home-return as one of the planned stops by reserving capacity (e.g., simulate stop_count-1 non-home stops then optionally add home as last stop), or (b) include the home-return hazard inside the step loop as a termination/next-action choice so the realized length matches the modeled distribution. Refit/retune return-home bias after enforcing budget.
reflection.key_insight: When a metric evaluates an output count, any post-hoc appends must be counted in the same budget; never add events after sampling a fixed count without compensating logic.

blueprint_refs: "distance sensitivity using POI coordinates"
"trip_distance_wasserstein"
"join lat/lon via poi_category_192021_longitude_latitude.json"

evidence.metrics: trip_distance_wasserstein=2.554704637987986; gt coord_match_rate=0.3010 vs sim coord_match_rate=0.2748 (validation meta)

reflection.error_identification: Trip distance distribution is notably mismatched (Wasserstein ~2.55 km units) while only ~27–30% of consecutive pairs have coordinates, meaning the metric is computed on a sparse, biased subset.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: When coords are missing, the simulator uses a fixed default_km=3.0 for distance penalties and travel-time proxy, making location choice and timing weakly coupled to geography for many moves. Additionally, candidates_for_category mixes 'nearest by anchor coords' with random fill of missing-coord POIs, creating a simulated coordinate-availability subset different from ground truth (coord_match_rate differs). The evaluated distance sample is thus biased and the control signal for calibration is noisy.
reflection.correct_approach: Improve distance handling under missing coords: (1) in _choose_next_poi, avoid applying distance penalties based on arbitrary default_km; instead down-weight distance terms when either endpoint lacks coords (e.g., treat as unknown and rely on preference/transition), or impute distances via category-level typical distance distributions learned from coord-available transitions. (2) In candidates_for_category, match ground-truth coordinate availability by sampling coord-known vs coord-unknown POIs in proportions closer to observed for that category/user. (3) In calibration/evaluation logs, report trip-distance metric together with coord_match_rate and matched_pairs counts and optionally optimize a composite that penalizes coord_match_rate drift.
reflection.key_insight: Distance metrics are only meaningful when the coord-available subset is representative; if coords are sparse, either impute distances from learned distributions or explicitly control/monitor coordinate-availability bias.

blueprint_refs: "time-of-day activity propensity"
"timestamps are rounded to nearest minute"
"Initialize at sampled start time; ... ensuring monotonic increase within day"

evidence.metrics: tod_jsd_avg=0.03148900009153471 (good overall), but sample outputs include very early 00:xx starts (e.g., 2021-09-23 Home#582 at 00:20:00)

reflection.error_identification: Although ToD JSD is decent on average, simulated days sometimes start at extreme times (00:xx) and produce long chains across the night, suggesting the model may be conflating late-night activity with next-day starts.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: The parser clamps non-monotone timestamps within the listed order (parse_day_record), but does not model cross-midnight wrap; any records that implicitly cross midnight can be coerced into non-decreasing times within the same day. This can inflate start_time_hist mass near 0 and push generated start times earlier than realistic for some users.
reflection.correct_approach: Detect and handle overnight wrap during parsing: when a time decreases relative to previous within a day string, interpret it as next-day continuation and either (a) drop wrapped visits from that day record, or (b) cap at 23:59 and stop parsing further visits, or (c) split into two day records if the dataset semantics allow (blueprint implies per-day records; safest is to stop parsing at first wrap). Recompute start_time_hist after this fix.
reflection.key_insight: For per-day trajectory models, never force non-monotone within-day times to be monotone by clamping; explicitly treat decreases as an overnight boundary event.

blueprint_refs: "category transition model"
"coarse category mapping via catto.json"
"day_of_week ... modulates category time-of-day propensity"

evidence.metrics: transition_divergence=0.02462610280070624 (good), category_share_mae=0.012408236925022818

reflection.error_identification: Transitions and category shares are already strong, but the implementation is partially misaligned with the blueprint’s coarse-category and exogenous modulation intent, limiting headroom and robustness.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Training transitions are built on fine categories only (fit_pattern_from_training uses v.category), while evaluation transitions are computed on coarse-mapped categories (Evaluator._collect_transitions uses to_coarse_category). Additionally, exogenous signals are only used for a narrow weekend office down-weight heuristic; month/seasonality is unused, and day-of-week does not modulate stop counts or time profiles as specified.
reflection.correct_approach: Align modeling space: either (a) learn transitions on coarse categories (apply to_coarse_category during pattern fitting) to match evaluation and blueprint, or (b) evaluate on fine categories consistently (but blueprint prefers coarse mapping when available). Extend exogenous conditioning: adjust time_of_day_category_profile and/or mixture weights by day_of_week and month (global learned multipliers from training), and optionally modulate stop_multiplier by weekend/weekday.
reflection.key_insight: Train/evaluate in the same categorical space; if evaluation uses coarse mapping, learn transitions/preferences in coarse space too, then layer fine POI choice underneath.

blueprint_refs: Motivation for a target day is computed from the past 7 observed days (not necessarily consecutive calendar days) prior to the target date within 2021 only.
validation targets ... ONLY keep validation dates d where the user has at least 7 prior observed days ... for Motivation computation

evidence.metrics: Validation metrics show ToD mismatch and stop-count errors (tod_jsd_avg=0.0511, stop_count_abs_mean_error=1.2907). Code computes motivation even when <7 prior days exist.

reflection.error_identification: Motivation is intended to be computed from exactly the past 7 observed days (when available), but the simulator uses whatever number of prior days exist (1–7) and silently falls back when none exist.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: MobilitySimulator._compute_motivation() takes prior_days[-7:] without asserting len(prior_days) >= 7 and returns non-neutral motivation for <7 days. Blueprint requires 7-day context for target-day conditioning; while validation split enforces >=7 for included targets, this function is also used during calibration rollouts and could condition on shorter histories if invoked outside the validated target set.
reflection.correct_approach: Enforce blueprint semantics in _compute_motivation(): if len(prior_days) < 7 return neutral motivation (multipliers/deltas = 0/1). Additionally, in rollout/generate_day, optionally assert that the passed-in target day meets the >=7 prior-days condition when simulating validation, to catch leakage/misuse early.
reflection.key_insight: If a feature is defined on a fixed-length history window, enforce the window length in code (assert or neutral fallback), otherwise you get distribution shift between calibration/validation and unstable behavior.

blueprint_refs: stops-per-day distribution ... Base rate is then modulated by Motivation computed from the past 7 days
policy: sample baseline number of stops ... from learned per-user distributions

evidence.metrics: stop_count_abs_mean_error=1.2907, stop_count_kl=0.7930 on validation.

reflection.error_identification: Simulated days have noticeably incorrect number-of-stops distribution and mean, despite fitting per-user stop_count_hist.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: _sample_stop_count() samples a base stop count from an empirical histogram but then applies an ad-hoc, capped adjustment: at most 3 integer steps plus a fractional step. This can systematically under/over-shoot the motivation-scaled target, especially for users whose empirical stop counts have wider support. In generate_day(), the loop may further truncate remaining_steps based on time budget, creating additional bias vs the sampled stop budget.
reflection.correct_approach: Replace the capped adjustment with a proper reweighting of the discrete histogram: construct a PMF over counts where log-prob is log(hist[count]) + lambda * count (or similar) to match the desired mean implied by stop_multiplier, then sample from that PMF. Also incorporate feasibility (time budget) by sampling a feasible count conditional on start time (e.g., reject-sample counts that exceed max_additional).
reflection.key_insight: When you need to modulate a discrete empirical distribution, reweight the whole distribution (exponential tilting) instead of post-hoc clamped nudges; clamping creates biased means and KL spikes.

blueprint_refs: trip_distance_wasserstein: join lat/lon via poi_category_192021_longitude_latitude.json; compute haversine distance between consecutive stops
distance sensitivity using POI coordinates

evidence.metrics: trip_distance_wasserstein=1.6615; coord_match_rate gt=0.3010 vs sim=0.2687; stdout: 'added 3858 trajectory-only POIs without coordinates'.

reflection.error_identification: Trip-distance distribution is still materially off, and the simulated coordinate-match rate is lower than ground truth, which changes which trips are even included in the metric computation.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Many observed POIs are added with lat/lon=None (load_data). Evaluator._trip_distances only includes legs where both endpoints have coordinates, so the evaluated distribution is conditional on coord-available trips. The simulator adds only a small coord_bonus (0.12) and defaults unknown distances to 2.0km in _travel_minutes, but it can still generate more coord-missing legs than the ground truth subset, changing both the sample set and the distance distribution. This is a metric/selection bias problem, not just a distance-penalty tuning problem.
reflection.correct_approach: Make coordinate availability an explicit part of candidate selection to match the metric’s conditioning: when current_poi has coords (or anchor has coords), restrict candidates to coord-known POIs with high probability (e.g., 0.8–0.95), falling back to coord-unknown only when category pool is empty. Separately, report and optimize against coord_match_rate internally (as a diagnostic, not a new required metric) to ensure the evaluated sample is comparable.
reflection.key_insight: If evaluation filters to coord-available legs, the simulator must match the same conditioning set; otherwise you are optimizing distance on a moving target.

blueprint_refs: Parse observed day-strings into structured trajectories (date, ordered visits with POI category, POI id, time).
timestamps are rounded to nearest minute for fitting and generation.

evidence.metrics: tod_jsd_avg=0.0511; sample outputs show many starts at 00:xx. parse_day_record stops parsing on first decreasing timestamp, potentially discarding late-day visits.

reflection.error_identification: Time-of-day fit remains imperfect and some generated days start extremely early (00:xx), indicating the learned start-time distribution may be contaminated; additionally, truncating parsed trajectories can distort stop counts and transitions.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: parse_day_record() interprets any within-record time decrease as an overnight wrap and breaks, discarding remaining visits. If the raw data contains small out-of-order timestamps (not true midnight crossings), this truncation removes visits, shifting the first-visit time earlier and reducing per-day stop counts used for training statistics (start_time_hist, stop_count_hist, transition_pmf).
reflection.correct_approach: Differentiate true midnight wrap from small ordering noise: only treat as overnight wrap if the decrease is large (e.g., previous >= 20:00 and next <= 04:00) or if a date boundary token exists (none here). Otherwise, skip the offending visit (continue) or clamp it to last_t+1 minute, and keep parsing. Log counts of (a) minor decreases skipped, (b) major decreases treated as wrap, to verify behavior on 1921Y.json.
reflection.key_insight: When parsing sequential time data, ‘monotone’ violations need a tolerance model; breaking on first violation silently corrupts downstream distributions.

blueprint_refs: coarse_category_map ... optional
encounter-driven category awareness ... additive term to the category utility

evidence.metrics: transition_divergence=0.0294 (good but not best), category_share_mae=0.0119 (good). Code applies awareness keyed by coarse category but multiplies probabilities for fine category tokens, potentially inconsistent across mapping.

reflection.error_identification: Social influence is intended to operate at coarse category level, but the model’s category sampling operates on fine categories while applying awareness via coarse mapping, which can unevenly amplify some fine categories depending on mapping coverage.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: In rollout(), awareness is stored on coarse categories. In _choose_next_category(), it computes coarse=to_coarse_category(c, ...) for each fine category c and uses aw from coarse. This causes all fine categories mapping to the same coarse to be boosted equally even if the time profile/transition pmf are fine-grained; additionally, fine categories not present in catto.json may never receive awareness boosts, creating inconsistent social effects.
reflection.correct_approach: Decide one resolution for category choice: either (a) sample in coarse space consistently (convert transition/time profiles to coarse at training time), or (b) keep fine sampling but store awareness in fine space (no mapping) and only map to coarse for evaluation. If keeping coarse awareness, distribute coarse awareness back to fine categories proportionally to their baseline probabilities within the coarse bucket.
reflection.key_insight: Do not mix aggregation levels inside a probabilistic choice model unless you explicitly define how mass transfers between levels; otherwise you introduce hidden, data-dependent biases.

blueprint_refs: Calibrate ... on the 2021 training period ... Validate on holdout dates (2021 only) and report goodness-of-fit metrics.

evidence.metrics: Best objective is selected on a 250-day training subsample; validation objective differs (training best_obj=3.2770 vs validation objective=2.8357).

reflection.error_identification: The calibration procedure is noisy and may pick parameters that fit a small subsample rather than generalizing, contributing to residual validation errors (notably stop count and trip distance).
reflection.root_cause_analysis: RandomSearchCalibrator.fit() evaluates each parameter set on a single deterministic subsample of 250 training days and uses a single rollout (no repeated seeds, no cross-validation within training). Given stochastic simulation and heterogeneity across users/days, the objective has high variance, so the chosen ‘best’ can be suboptimal on full validation.
reflection.correct_approach: Reduce objective variance: evaluate each proposed parameter set on multiple subsamples (or bootstrap) and average objective, or increase calibration_day_budget. Also reset agents’ transient category_awareness before each evaluation rollout to avoid cross-iteration contamination of the objective (currently awareness persists across rollouts inside the calibration loop).
reflection.key_insight: Stochastic simulators need low-variance calibration signals (multiple rollouts / resets / larger samples); otherwise random search overfits noise.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "treat these as the resident's Pattern/Persona representations" and "STRICTLY pre-target when generating targets"
Blueprint: "Motivation for a target day is computed from the past 7 observed days prior to the target date"

evidence.metrics: Validation metrics are decent but non-trivial errors remain (stop_count_abs_mean_error=0.388, stop_count_kl=0.224, tod_jsd_avg=0.0275, trip_distance_wasserstein=0.364), consistent with using a single per-user pattern fit from all training days rather than recomputing per target day using only days < target.

reflection.error_identification: Pattern/anchors are fit once per user from the whole training split, then reused for every validation target day. Blueprint requires Pattern/Persona/Motivation to be computed strictly from days before each target date (no future leakage within 2021).
reflection.root_cause_analysis: In main(), patterns are fit from split.train_by_user[uid] once and reused; there is no per-target recomputation using only training days earlier than each validation date. This violates the per-target preconditioning requirement and can bias start-time/stop-count/transition distributions for later validation dates.
reflection.correct_approach: For each (user, validation_day) during rollout, derive an effective training subset = {d in 2021 | d < validation_day} (and optionally also within the user's training portion) and fit/refresh the agent’s Pattern (anchors, histograms, transitions) from that subset before generating that day. Cache by (user, cutoff_day) or by validation day to avoid recomputing from scratch. Ensure Motivation uses only the prior 7 observed days < target_day (already enforced) and that Pattern uses only < target_day too.
reflection.key_insight: When the spec says "strictly pre-target", treat Pattern/Persona as time-indexed state: either recompute per target cutoff or maintain incremental sufficient statistics; don’t reuse a single fit across multiple target dates.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "modeling in minutes" and "timestamps are rounded to nearest minute for fitting and generation"
Blueprint policy: "time-of-day activity propensity" and timing conditioned on Pattern/Persona/Motivation

evidence.metrics: tod_jsd_avg=0.0275 suggests remaining mismatch in arrival time distribution; model uses coarse 15-minute bin profiles and a simple start-time histogram without a learned within-day intensity process.

reflection.error_identification: Time-of-day matching is only indirectly controlled (15-min global category profile + per-user start-time histogram + lognormal dwell). This can leave systematic ToD divergence even when category/transition distributions look good.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: The simulator does not fit a per-user or global arrival-time hazard/intensity over minutes; it uses a coarse profile at 15-minute bins and then advances time by dwell+travel. This limits the model’s ability to reproduce multi-peak daily rhythms and late-night/early-morning structure.
reflection.correct_approach: Keep minute resolution, but replace or augment the 15-min profile with (a) per-user (or persona) arrival intensity over finer bins (e.g., 5 minutes) and/or (b) a learned inter-event time distribution conditioned on category and time-of-day. Concretely: estimate empirical inter-arrival gaps by category and time bin from training; sample next gap from that conditional distribution rather than only dwell+travel. Maintain monotonic time constraint.
reflection.key_insight: To match ToD distributions, controlling only start-time + dwell is often insufficient; add an explicit arrival-time or inter-arrival model conditioned on time-of-day.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "trip distance distributions ... join lat/lon via poi_category_192021_longitude_latitude.json"
Blueprint: "distance sensitivity using POI coordinates"

evidence.metrics: trip_distance_wasserstein=0.3639; evaluator meta shows coord_match_rate gt=0.3010 sim=0.2975, and load logs report 3858 trajectory-only POIs without coordinates were added.

reflection.error_identification: Trip-distance metric is computed only on legs where both POIs have coordinates; the pipeline injects many coordinate-missing POIs and also uses distance fallbacks when coords are missing, which can distort both behavior and evaluation comparability.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: In load_data(), any POI token seen in trajectories but missing from the POI file is inserted with lat/lon=None, expanding candidate pools with coord-missing POIs. In _choose_next_poi and _travel_minutes, missing distances are replaced by a small fallback (capped by 5km), which compresses simulated distance variability and can bias the Wasserstein distance even if coord_match_rate is similar.
reflection.correct_approach: Separate concerns: (1) keep missing-coord POIs for output validity, but (2) when the metric conditions on coord-known legs, explicitly prefer coord-known POIs for non-anchor moves when current/anchor has coords (already partially done) and (3) raise the fallback distance distribution to match observed coord-known distance statistics (e.g., sample fallback distances from the empirical gt distance distribution conditional on category/time rather than a fixed <=5km cap). Additionally, report and optionally optimize on coord_match_rate in calibration meta (without inventing a new official metric).
reflection.key_insight: If evaluation conditions on data availability (coords), your simulator must (a) control the missingness mechanism and (b) make fallback values distributional, not constant, or you will bias distance metrics.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "return-home hazard params" and "Calibrate to match distribution of last-visit times"

evidence.metrics: tod_jsd_avg=0.0275 and stop_count metrics (abs_mean_error=0.388, kl=0.224) can be impacted by an overly simplistic end-of-day/home-return process; sample outputs show frequent very-late activities and sometimes multiple late-night stops.

reflection.error_identification: The model’s end-of-day behavior is governed by a simple late-time linear factor and a single bias; this may not match observed last-visit-time distributions and can push stop counts and ToD tails off-target.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: generate_day() adds an optional home return only if simulated visits < stop_count, with p_home based on (t-18:00) and return_home_bias. There is no calibrated hazard over time that can end the day early, nor is there a last-activity-time objective term; calibration therefore may fit stop counts but miss last-visit timing structure.
reflection.correct_approach: Implement an explicit per-step stopping/return-home hazard that depends on current time (and optionally cumulative fatigue) and can terminate generation before reaching the planned stop_count, then fit its parameters using training last-visit-time statistics (or include ToD tail penalties indirectly). Keep blueprint constraint that Motivation can modulate this hazard.
reflection.key_insight: To match ToD tails and realistic day endings, you need a stopping process; a single post-hoc ‘maybe return home’ rule is usually insufficient.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "Calibrate ... on 2021 training period" and "Validate on holdout dates"

evidence.metrics: Calibration best objective on training subsample=0.7073 vs validation objective=0.9076; calibration iter objectives vary widely (e.g., 0.707 to 8.04), indicating a noisy optimization surface even with rng reset.

reflection.error_identification: Calibration uses a 250-day subsample and random-search parameters; large objective swings suggest sensitivity to parameter proposals and dataset subsampling, and the best-on-subsample parameters do not fully transfer to validation.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: The calibrator optimizes on a fixed-size random subsample of training days and uses a simple random search without stratification by user/date or multiple rollouts per parameter set. This can overfit the subsample and yield high-variance objective estimates.
reflection.correct_approach: Stabilize calibration signal: (1) stratify subsampling across users and across time (early/late 2021), (2) evaluate each proposed parameter set on multiple disjoint subsamples or bootstrap splits and average the objective, (3) optionally narrow parameter ranges (e.g., travel_time_scale) based on data-derived priors to reduce variance.
reflection.key_insight: If the optimizer is random-search and the rollout is stochastic, reduce variance first (stratified subsamples + replicate evaluations) before increasing search complexity.

evidence.metrics: Execution succeeded in this run, but the script includes OpenAI API calls and env requirements that are unrelated to the mobility simulation pipeline and could fail in other environments.

reflection.error_identification: The file contains unrelated OpenAI/LLM code paths that can raise environment/import errors if accidentally triggered and adds maintenance risk.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: OpenAI helper functions and prompt builders are present at top-level in the same script as the simulator, despite not being used by main(). This is dead code for the blueprint and can cause confusion and potential runtime failures if called.
reflection.correct_approach: Remove the unused OpenAI helper block from this simulation script (or gate it behind a CLI flag and ensure it is never imported/executed in the default run). Keep the simulator self-contained and data-driven per blueprint.
reflection.key_insight: Don’t ship unrelated optional dependencies inside the core execution path; isolate optional tooling to avoid accidental failures and simplify reproducibility.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "Pattern/Persona to be computed strictly from days prior to each target date"
Blueprint: "Motivation ... from the past 7 observed days prior to the target date ... within 2021 only (no future leakage)"
holdout_plan: "Pattern/Persona/Motivation must be computed using only days strictly before each target date."

evidence.metrics: Calibration uses simulator.rollout(train_by_user) where agents.available_training_days=train_by_user; generate_day fits patterns on all days < target day even during training objective, causing per-day conditioning to vary with target day and making the objective different from the later 'fit_pattern_from_training(all train days)' initialization step. Validation metrics are good, but this mismatch can inflate calibration variance and lead to unstable parameter selection (seen in calibration_history objectives ranging ~1.94 to ~5.65).

reflection.error_identification: Training-time calibration objective is internally inconsistent: agents are pre-fit on the full training set, but each generated training day re-fits/overrides the pattern to a per-day cutoff via ensure_pattern_upto(day). This creates a moving target objective and higher variance across parameter proposals.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: In RandomSearchCalibrator.fit/BaseCalibrator.fit, agents.fit_pattern_from_training(train_by_user[uid]) is called once, but MobilitySimulator.generate_day always calls agent.ensure_pattern_upto(day, env), which replaces agent.pattern based on cutoff_day using agent.available_training_days. This leads to per-day pattern differences during calibration that are not mirrored by the single global fit and are not explicitly modeled/controlled as part of the calibrator design.
reflection.correct_approach:
Make calibration objective consistent with the intended conditioning strategy:
- Option A (preferred): remove the one-time fit_pattern_from_training() inside calibrators, and rely entirely on ensure_pattern_upto() during rollout; also clear each agent’s _pattern_cache at the start of each calibration iteration to avoid cross-iteration contamination.
- Option B: for training-calibration only, disable ensure_pattern_upto() and use a fixed pattern fit on all training days, but then also compute Motivation only from days < target to avoid leakage; this is less aligned with blueprint’s per-target requirement.
Additionally, log per-iteration cache hit rate and average number of prior days used to verify consistency.
reflection.key_insight: When a model uses per-target pre-cutoff fitting, calibration must use the exact same per-target fitting semantics (and cache lifecycle) as evaluation; otherwise the optimizer is fitting to a different generator than the one you report.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "trip distance distributions ... join lat/lon via poi_category_192021_longitude_latitude.json"
Blueprint: "distance sensitivity using POI coordinates"

code_refs.symbol: load_data (POI catalog: added trajectory-only POIs without coordinates)

evidence.metrics: trip_distance_wasserstein=0.7591, while evaluator_meta shows coord_match_rate only ~0.30 for both gt and sim (gt matched_pairs_with_coords=1400/4651, sim=1315/4325). Also stdout: "POI catalog: added 3858 trajectory-only POIs without coordinates".

reflection.error_identification: Trip-distance evaluation is computed on a small, potentially biased subset of legs where both endpoints have coordinates (~30% match rate). The reported Wasserstein distance can be dominated by which legs are 'eligible' rather than the true movement distribution.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Evaluator._trip_distances drops any consecutive pair where env.distance_km(a,b) is None. Meanwhile, load_data injects thousands of POIs without coordinates into poi_catalog, which increases the number of dropped legs. The simulator also uses sampled fallback distances for dynamics, but those fallbacks do not contribute to the evaluation distance set because evaluation uses only coordinate-known pairs.
reflection.correct_approach:
Improve robustness/interpretability of the distance metric without inventing new APIs:
- In Evaluator.compute_metrics, include coord_match_rate in the top-level reported metrics/meta and treat very low match rates as a warning (e.g., attach to evaluation_payload.meta).
- During simulation, increase probability of selecting POIs with known coordinates when distance-sensitive utilities are active (e.g., in POIEnvironment.candidates_for_category and MobilitySimulator._choose_next_poi: upweight coord-known POIs beyond the current small coord_bonus).
- Optionally (within current metric definition constraints), compute distances for missing-coordinate legs using the same fallback prior and report it as meta only; do not mix into the official metric unless the spec allows.
reflection.key_insight: If an evaluation metric relies on an external join (POI→coords), always track and act on the join coverage; otherwise the metric may be measuring data availability, not model quality.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "dwell-time / inter-event-time model"
Blueprint: "timestamps are rounded to nearest minute for fitting and generation"
Blueprint: "sample dwell time and add travel-time proxy based on distance"

evidence.metrics: stop_count_abs_mean_error=0.4738 (moderate), stop_count_kl=0.3417 (moderate), tod_jsd_avg=0.02636 (good but non-zero). Calibration found travel_time_scale≈1.18 and routine_strength≈0.025, suggesting the model compensates for timing via other knobs.

reflection.error_identification: The simulator reaches decent ToD and stop-count metrics but requires extreme calibration outcomes (very low routine_strength, very low travel_time_scale), indicating compensating behavior and potential brittleness out of sample.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Dwell means are estimated from inter-visit gaps (_compute_dwell_means_by_category), but generation adds both dwell and travel (dwell sampled lognormal around that mean, plus travel_time_scale*distance). This double-counts gap structure: the estimated 'dwell mean' already contains travel time, so the generator can inflate time advances and reduce feasible stops unless travel_time_scale collapses toward 0. This coupling forces calibration into corner solutions.
reflection.correct_approach:
Decouple dwell and travel proxies using only existing fields:
- When computing dwell_time_mean_by_category, subtract an estimated travel component for coordinate-known pairs (e.g., use a default travel_time_scale_guess and distance, then clip at >=5 minutes) before averaging; keep as a proxy, not perfect.
- Alternatively, set travel_time_scale fixed (or narrow range) and let dwell means absorb most of the gap; calibrate only one of {dwell_mean_scale, travel_time_scale} to avoid identifiability issues.
- Add logging of average (dwell, travel) contributions and average inter-event gap to validate that simulated gaps match observed.
reflection.key_insight: If you estimate dwell from inter-event gaps, you must avoid adding a second travel term at generation time unless you explicitly remove travel from the dwell estimate; otherwise parameters become unidentifiable and optimizers push to degenerate corners.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "sample next category using a convex mixture of (a) transition matrix ... and (b) time-of-day category propensity"
Blueprint: "routine_strength ... modulated by Motivation"

evidence.metrics: Calibration chose routine_strength≈0.0245 but transition_divergence is already low (0.0211), suggesting category transitions are being matched largely via other signals (time profile, preferences).

reflection.error_identification: The optimizer drives routine_strength near zero even though transition matching is good, a sign that the global time-of-day profile and other priors may be overpowering personalized transition structure.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: MobilitySimulator._choose_next_category mixes per-user transition_pmf and a global time profile. The global profile is computed across all users and can dominate when transitions are sparse or smoothed uniformly. With low routine_strength, the model uses the global profile, which can still yield low transition_divergence at coarse-category level while reducing individual realism.
reflection.correct_approach:
Strengthen individualized structure without new data sources:
- Add a small prior toward agent.pattern.category_pref in the mixture (e.g., blend prof with category_pref before mixing with transitions), so that even when routine_strength is low, choices remain personalized.
- Reduce global profile dominance by smoothing it less aggressively for rare categories or by limiting it to coarse categories only (consistent mapping already exists).
- Log per-user transition coverage (#states with transition_pmf) to confirm whether low routine_strength is compensating for sparse transitions.
reflection.key_insight: When combining a global prior with per-user dynamics, monitor for parameter collapse (e.g., routine_strength→0); it often means the global signal is too strong or the per-user signal is too sparse to compete.

blueprint_refs: STRICT: "Pattern/Persona/Motivation must be computed using only days strictly before each target date."
"For each target (validation) day, compute Motivation features strictly from the resident's past 7 observed days prior to the target date"

evidence.metrics: validation metrics are strong overall (topk_poi_recall=0.92), but current implementation can still violate per-target pre-date conditioning when target_day is inside the training set.

reflection.error_identification: Blueprint requires per-target-day Pattern/Persona/Motivation computed strictly from days < target day. Current calibration/training rollout uses agent.available_training_days=train_by_user and then ensure_pattern_upto(day) filters within that set; but when evaluating training days, the cutoff is within the same train set and should exclude future training days after the cutoff. The code currently does exclude days >= cutoff in ensure_pattern_upto, but Motivation uses the same source and is only neutral if <7 prior days; this can still mismatch the Blueprint’s ‘past 7 observed days prior to target day’ requirement if the per-target context should include ALL observed days prior to that training target, not just the preselected 80% training split.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Design decision conflates two different contexts: (1) per-user train/val split, and (2) per-target pre-date context. In BaseCalibrator.fit and RandomSearchCalibrator.fit, agent.available_training_days is set to split.train_by_user[uid] (earliest 80% dates). For any target day inside that set, the Blueprint’s pre-target context should be ‘all observed days before that date’ (still within 2021), not restricted to the train split subset. This affects both ResidentAgent.ensure_pattern_upto() and MobilitySimulator._compute_motivation().
reflection.correct_approach: During calibration rollouts over training days, set the per-target context source to the user’s full 2021 observed history (agent.observed_2021), but enforce that the *loss* uses only training targets. Concretely: do not overwrite agent.available_training_days with train_by_user for calibration; instead add a separate guard that prevents using validation targets as rollouts, while allow ensure_pattern_upto(day) to see any observed day < day (including those that would be in validation if they are earlier than the current training target). Keep the validation rollout as-is (where only days < validation target exist by definition).
reflection.key_insight: Keep ‘which days are targets for optimization’ separate from ‘which past days are allowed as conditioning context’. Train/val split is about targets; pre-target conditioning is about time ordering.

blueprint_refs: "sample baseline number of stops ... then ... apply Motivation ..."
"Decrease with total time ... influences probability to return home/end day."

evidence.metrics: stop_count_abs_mean_error=0.7296511627906979, stop_count_kl=0.36325244135292295

reflection.error_identification: Stop counts are still noticeably off (mean absolute error ~0.73 stops/day; KL ~0.36), indicating systematic bias rather than noise.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: generate_day() samples a target stop_count, but then reduces realized stops via: (a) feasibility clamp using min_gap=5 and remaining_budget (reasonable), (b) a late-day stopping hazard p_stop that can trigger once len(visits)>=2, and (c) hard break when t_next>1439. This creates a consistent under-shoot of the sampled stop_count, and the hazard is only time/progress-based (not calibrated against the per-user stop_count_hist or last-visit-time statistics).
reflection.correct_approach: Make realized stop count consistent with the sampled stop_count unless the day truly runs out of time: remove/disable the stochastic early-stop hazard for most of the day, or calibrate it to match the empirical probability of ending before completing planned stops. Practical patch: only allow early termination once (t >= 20:00) AND (len(visits) >= stop_count-1), or compute p_stop from remaining_time vs expected dwell+travel so it triggers mainly when infeasible. Then re-check stop_count_kl/abs error.
reflection.key_insight: If you sample a discrete budget (stops), don’t also add an uncalibrated termination process that systematically truncates the budget; tie termination to feasibility or fit it to data.

blueprint_refs: "trip distance distributions (computed from consecutive-stop geographic distances)"
"Link POI ids to coordinates ..."

code_refs.symbol: Evaluator._trip_distances + load_data (adding missing POIs without coords) + MobilitySimulator._distance_km_or_fallback

evidence.metrics: trip_distance_wasserstein=0.6013982123108319; coord_match_rate gt=0.3010 sim=0.2924

reflection.error_identification: Trip-distance evaluation is based on only ~30% of legs having coordinates (gt coord_match_rate≈0.30; sim≈0.29). This makes trip_distance_wasserstein less informative and can hide distance model problems or induce unstable calibration behavior.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: load_data() adds many trajectory-only POIs with lat/lon=None (stdout: added 3858). Simulator chooses POIs without requiring coords; when coords are missing, generation uses sampled fallback distances for timing/utility, but evaluation ignores those legs entirely. Even with the enforced coord-known share in _choose_next_poi, sim coord_match_rate remains ~0.29.
reflection.correct_approach: Increase coordinate match rate beyond ~0.3 by tightening candidate selection when coordinates are available: in _choose_next_poi(), when there exists any coord-known POI in the category, restrict pool to coord-known POIs (or set min_coord_share closer to 0.85–0.95), and only fall back to coord-unknown if the category has no coords. This directly increases matched_pairs_with_coords in Evaluator._trip_distances, improving the signal and typically reducing trip_distance_wasserstein variance.
reflection.key_insight: If a metric is computed on a joinable subset (needs coordinates), ensure the simulator generates joinable outputs; otherwise the metric becomes a low-power, biased diagnostic.

blueprint_refs: "Encounter probability can be a function of POI coarse category (if available)."
"awareness boost ... additive term to category utility and decays over time"

evidence.metrics: topk_poi_recall=0.92, transition_divergence=0.0234 (good), but current mechanism can over-amplify some categories depending on mapping collisions

reflection.error_identification: Encounter-driven awareness is added to both fine_cat and coarse category keys during rollout, and then _choose_next_category applies awareness by checking fine first and then coarse. This can effectively stack boosts when fine==coarse or when both keys are present for the same semantic category.
reflection.root_cause_analysis:
In rollout(), for each encounter POI the code does:
- ag.category_awareness[fine_cat] += 0.05
- ag.category_awareness[coarse] += 0.05
Then in _choose_next_category() it reads:
aw = agent.category_awareness.get(c, agent.category_awareness.get(coarse, 0.0))
This mixes levels inconsistently: if c is already coarse (because categories are not mapped consistently), the boost can be applied in unintended ways.
reflection.correct_approach: Choose one representation for awareness (prefer coarse categories, since Blueprint suggests coarse-category dependence). Patch: only store awareness under coarse category, and in _choose_next_category() always lookup by to_coarse_category(c). If you need fine-grained, store fine only and never also store coarse for the same event.
reflection.key_insight: Don’t store the same signal at multiple taxonomy levels unless you also enforce a non-overlapping lookup rule; otherwise you create hidden amplification.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "Motivation for a target day is computed from the past 7 observed days ... prior to the target date within 2021 only."
Blueprint holdout_plan: "Pattern/Persona/Motivation must be computed using only days strictly before each target date."

evidence.metrics: Validation metrics computed on 688 user-days with requires_full_7day_context=true; code restricts available_training_days to split.train_by_user for validation.

reflection.error_identification: Validation generation conditions Pattern/Motivation on only the training-split history (agent.available_training_days = split.train_by_user[uid]) rather than on all observed days strictly prior to each validation target date. This deviates from the Blueprint conditioning rule and can alter generated stop/time distributions.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: In main(), before validation rollout, the code sets ag.available_training_days = split.train_by_user.get(uid). Then ensure_pattern_upto() and _compute_motivation() use available_training_days as the sole context source. This enforces an additional constraint ("train-history only") not in the Blueprint, and can exclude legitimate pre-target observed days that lie in the validation tail but still precede later validation targets.
reflection.correct_approach: For each validation target day d, build context = {all observed 2021 days < d} (not future days, but not restricted to the train split). Remove the global available_training_days assignment for validation, and instead enforce no-leakage via the strict cutoff check already implemented (d < target_day). If you still want a stricter holdout, reflect it explicitly in the Blueprint/metrics design and evaluate accordingly.
reflection.key_insight: Don’t conflate temporal leakage prevention with “train-split-only” conditioning; enforce leakage by cutoff_day (< target) rather than by dataset split membership.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint metric: "trip_distance_wasserstein" uses poi_category_192021_longitude_latitude.json joins.
Blueprint: "Link POI ids to coordinates ... by matching the exact 'Category#id' string."

code_refs.symbol: load_data (adds missing POIs with lat/lon None)

evidence.metrics: trip_distance_wasserstein=0.6139401218267196; gt coord_match_rate=0.3010 (1400/4651), sim coord_match_rate=0.2973 (1341/4510).

reflection.error_identification: Trip-distance evaluation is based on only ~30% of consecutive-stop pairs having coordinates; Wasserstein distance is therefore computed on a small, biased subset and remains relatively high.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: load_data() logs show 3858 trajectory-only POIs are injected with lat/lon None, and the simulation can still select them (especially under exploration). Although _choose_next_poi() tries to increase coord-known share, it only hard-restricts when coord_candidates>=50 and otherwise attempts a heuristic min_coord_share, leaving many missing-coord POIs in circulation. Evaluator._trip_distances drops missing-coord legs entirely, reducing effective sample size.
reflection.correct_approach: Make coordinate availability a first-class constraint for distance-evaluable legs: (1) during candidate building, prefer coord-known POIs whenever the current_poi has coords and the category has any coord-known POIs; (2) when exploration selects unknown POIs, consider a two-stage: explore among coord-known first, then fallback to unknown only if no coord-known exist; (3) add a diagnostic report: per-category coord coverage and fraction of simulated stops/legs using coord-known POIs.
reflection.key_insight: If a metric depends on an external join (coords), ensure the generator’s sampling process matches that join’s support; otherwise evaluation becomes low-power and biased.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "daily mobility trajectories" with timing dynamics; start time distribution derived from first visit time per day.

evidence.metrics: tod_jsd_avg=0.025593439874026805 (good overall), but sample outputs show frequent 00:00–05:00 starts and dense overnight activity (e.g., 00:10, 00:30, 01:10).

reflection.error_identification: Despite low aggregate ToD divergence, many simulated days begin around midnight or very early morning, which may be unrealistic for typical daily mobility and can be an artifact of parsing/conditioning.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: The start-time histogram is taken directly from observed first-visit time (ResidentAgent.fit_pattern_from_training), but parsing (parse_day_record) can treat out-of-order times by clamping and can stop on overnight wraps. If the raw strings contain late-night continuation logs, the “first listed visit” may be after midnight even when conceptually part of the previous day; the code currently breaks on certain overnight patterns rather than reassigning them, and this can skew start_time_hist toward early minutes. Simulation then samples directly from this skewed histogram.
reflection.correct_approach: Introduce a defensible day-boundary rule for start-time estimation: when a day record contains a first timestamp in [00:00, 04:00] but later contains a timestamp >=20:00 (or indicates wrap), treat that early segment as carryover and exclude it from start_time_hist (or shift it to previous day) for pattern fitting. Additionally, clamp simulated start times to a user-specific central range (e.g., between 4th and 96th percentile of observed starts) to prevent pathological tails from dominating.
reflection.key_insight: Time-of-day distributions are extremely sensitive to day-boundary parsing; build explicit wrap-handling rules for “first stop” features rather than inheriting raw ordering artifacts.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "estimate P(next_category | current_category) from adjacent visits ... apply smoothing."

evidence.metrics: transition_divergence=0.02031488911651167 (good), but simulation outputs show occasional implausible jumps and repeated niche categories; smoothing currently adds +1 mass to every observed category for each c0.

reflection.error_identification: Transition smoothing currently distributes probability mass across all categories observed for the user, even if a category never follows a given current category; this can inject unrealistic transitions (especially for users with many distinct categories).
reflection.root_cause_analysis: In ResidentAgent.fit_pattern_from_training(), for each current category c0, the code iterates over all_cats and sets val = nexts.get(c1,0)+1, yielding dense transition distributions. This is a very strong smoothing scheme that can overwhelm the empirical next-category structure when all_cats is large. _choose_next_category() then mixes this transition pmf with global/user priors, potentially amplifying the dense tail.
reflection.correct_approach: Change smoothing to be sparse and conditional: only smooth over (a) categories observed as successors of c0 plus (b) a small backoff set (e.g., top-N user categories or global time-profile categories for the current ToD bin). Use a smaller additive alpha (e.g., 0.1) and/or interpolation with a backoff distribution rather than making every row dense.
reflection.key_insight: Additive smoothing over the full category vocabulary turns personalized Markov rows into near-uniform noise; smooth over a principled backoff set and interpolate instead of densifying.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "awareness boost ... decays over time ... within the same or next day."

evidence.metrics: No direct failure; calibration does reset awareness, but rollout itself does not enforce a hard reset and relies on external callers.

reflection.error_identification: Awareness is intended to be short-lived; correctness currently depends on callers remembering to reset before each rollout. This is fragile and can produce inconsistent results if rollout() is used elsewhere.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: MobilitySimulator.rollout() updates and decays agent.category_awareness but does not internally reset at the start; resets happen in BaseCalibrator.fit(), RandomSearchCalibrator.fit(), and main() validation setup. Any future reuse of rollout() without these resets will carry state across experiments/days beyond the intended mechanism.
reflection.correct_approach: Add an optional argument to rollout() like reset_awareness: bool = True, and when true call reset_all_awareness(self.agents) at rollout start. Keep the external resets too, but make rollout safe-by-default.
reflection.key_insight: Stateful social effects should be reset or scoped within the function that simulates them; don’t rely on external orchestration for correctness.

When a metric is used as a calibration signal, implement it exactly per definition; a subtly mis-specified denominator/truncation can collapse the signal (here: always near 0), making calibration chase the wrong objective.
Never let the declared format_spec diverge from emitted strings; keep a single canonical serializer and have parsers accept supersets for robustness.
Calibration optimizes exactly what you measure; if your metric suite drifts from spec, you’ll tune the wrong behavior even if the simulator ‘looks good’ on internal metrics.

When a divergence metric saturates at a constant across parameter sweeps, treat it as a taxonomy/support mismatch or a collapsed feature extractor; add overlap diagnostics and ensure identical representation for GT vs SIM.
If GT-derived trajectory stats (like step counts) are zero, debug parsing/joining before tuning the generator; otherwise all spatial metrics become meaningless.
When outputs are consumed by external evaluators, treat key names as API contracts; add backward-compatible aliases rather than renaming fields outright.

When the evaluation requires exact POI token matches, any purely “user-history-only” POI preference will collapse recall under distribution shift; always include global/popularity smoothing and candidate-set pruning.
Don’t silently substitute evaluation metrics: implement blueprint metrics directly, and only add extras as explicitly non-contract diagnostics.
For circular/ordered domains like time-of-day, prefer distance-aware metrics (EMD/Wasserstein) over divergence on coarse bins when the spec demands shift magnitude.
Match the metric family in the spec: MAE on shares and JSD capture different failures; using the wrong one can miscalibrate the shift parameters.

If you sample a target sequence length but later time-feasibility truncates the sequence, you must either enforce feasibility during length sampling or adjust gaps to honor the sampled length; otherwise stop-count metrics will be systematically biased.
Distance-based metrics are only as good as the POI-token join; always implement robust normalization and log unmatched identifiers before trusting mobility-radius results.
Context windows in OOD simulations must be time-bounded even in fallback paths; otherwise you silently change the conditioning regime and may inflate/deflate metrics unpredictably.
When output filenames are fixed by contract, make the evaluated split unambiguous in the JSON payload to prevent accidental ‘validation vs test’ misuse downstream.
Treat metric names as API: if the spec lists metrics, compute them explicitly with exact names and avoid burying them only in diagnostics.
If the evaluation includes exact-POI overlap, POI sampling must be conditioned (category → POI) and should include a high-mass component on the user’s frequent POIs within that category.
To match count MAE and count KL simultaneously, use a principled exponential tilt/temperature transform of the baseline PMF rather than shifting only the mean with arbitrary penalties.

Coarse start-time bins plus unconditioned gaps usually produce correct-looking averages but fail distributional metrics; add conditioning on stop index/transition to recover realistic temporal structure.
When a spec says “JSD”, using MAE instead can hide mode-collapse/mass-shift errors; match the divergence family required by the evaluation.
Spatial metrics are only meaningful when coordinate coverage is comparable; normalize tokens early and compute distance metrics on matched-coordinate subsets.
If a spec provides an online context window, use it to condition multiple parts of generation (counts, categories, POIs), not just the starting location.

If the blueprint allows an optional aggregation (super-category), implement both and clearly report which one drives selection; otherwise you risk optimizing an easier proxy metric that hides failures.
A context window should influence more than the initial state; use it to bias the full choice set toward recently seen user-specific POIs to improve recall under regime shifts.
Stop count, gaps, and day time budget must be jointly consistent; sampling them independently and then clipping times tends to inflate count errors and distort temporal statistics.
Avoid post-hoc rejection rules on learned temporal distributions; encode constraints directly into the fitted distribution or you will create distribution shifts that show up as TOD divergence.
If a metric depends on external joins, missingness can dominate; measure and equalize join success across simulated and GT before trusting distribution distances.
Don’t silently change calibrated parameter bounds; calibration depends on the search space matching the spec.

If the evaluation target is at POI token granularity, generating only higher-level categories will often look good on aggregate mix metrics while failing identity/recall metrics; model the finest target level explicitly.

Any feature derived from the labeled/evaluation-period ground truth (even if temporally earlier) must be treated as privileged information unless the task explicitly provides it as observed input at inference time.

Matching only the mean of a discrete behavioral distribution is often insufficient under regime shift; add at least one parameter controlling dispersion/tail weight and validate the realized histogram after all feasibility constraints.
Recall@K for sets is hurt by within-day repetition; enforce diversity or diminishing returns when sampling discrete items for a fixed-length list.
Feasibility clamping after independent gap sampling often reshapes the time distribution; generate time structure in a constraint-aware way rather than fixing it post hoc.
When a metric depends on optional metadata (coords), calibration becomes unreliable unless you control for missingness symmetry and log coverage/eligibility explicitly.
High-dimensional calibration with noisy objectives needs either many trials or structured/staged search; small random searches mostly measure noise.
Do not swap evaluation metrics for convenient proxies: implement required metrics exactly (or emit both with clear names) to avoid optimizing the wrong objective.
Calibration objectives must match the spec’s metric definitions (not just “similar” statistics), otherwise optimization will produce good-looking numbers that don’t satisfy the actual task.
OOD context windows need deterministic, spec-compliant backfill rules; arbitrary caps create silent distribution shifts and undermine personalization.
For POI-level recall tasks, controlling candidate set size is as important as weighting—high-cardinality categories require pruning/structuring to avoid probability dilution.
If you calibrate a multiplier on a discrete outcome, ensure the base distribution has enough support; otherwise optimization can’t express the needed shift.
Metric definitions shape what the simulator learns; prefix-based recall silently encourages ordering hacks rather than true day-level overlap.
Strict split hygiene includes clear artifact naming—mislabeling validation vs test can invalidate the experimental protocol even if the code is correct.
When calibration is black-box, reduce per-trial cost with deterministic subsampling/Successive Halving; better exploration often beats minor model tweaks.
If per-day MAE is high but distribution-level KL is low, the generator is matching marginals but missing conditional structure; add conditioning/dispersion controls rather than only mean scaling.
POI recall is dominated by category correctness and candidate-set sharpness; fix the first-stop category consistency and shrink candidate pools before tuning weights.
Never hide unit conversions behind legacy metric names; calibration will optimize the wrong scale and comparisons become meaningless.
Metric-key compatibility hacks must be audited with a unit test that asserts each output key equals the intended computed quantity.
OOD context windows should be defined in calendar time first; only then backfill to handle missingness—otherwise you inadvertently condition on arbitrary long-history slices.
Always guard side-effectful entrypoints; evaluation systems often import modules and expect zero execution on import.
